XRO.heroDescription: Cloud Accounting • 4.6M Subscribers • NZ$2.10B Revenue • AI Sell-Off Target
XRO.heroCompanyDescription: Xero Limited (ASX: XRO) is a New Zealand-founded, ASX-listed cloud accounting platform serving 4.6 million subscribers globally. The stock has become ground zero for the AI disruption repricing of SaaS: down 63% from its A$194 high to A$78.50, with the ASX tech sector collapsing 9.4% in a single session triggered by AI capability demonstrations (Anthropic Claude specifically cited as catalyst). Analyst consensus remains unanimously Buy with an average target of A$191, representing 164% upside. Either the market is right and AI will structurally impair cloud accounting, or this is the widest dislocation between price and fundamentals in Xero's listed history.

skew.rationale: The sell-off has now reached 63% on zero evidence of actual churn, customer loss, or revenue deceleration. Every analyst rates Buy. AI disruption fears are legitimate as a long-term structural question, but the market is pricing terminal impairment of a business that just delivered 20% revenue growth, historic low churn, and Rule of 40 at 44.5%. At A$78.50, the risk-reward skews decisively upside: unless AI displaces accounting software faster than any technology transition in history.

verdict.text: Xero has become the defining test case for the AI-disrupts-SaaS thesis. The stock is down 63% from its highs: an acceleration from the 55% decline just weeks ago: after an ASX tech sector rout triggered by Anthropic's Claude AI demonstrations that the market interpreted as threatening to cloud accounting moats. But here are the facts: revenue grew 20%, ARPU grew 15%, churn is at historic lows, Rule of 40 is 44.5%, and all 15 covering analysts rate Buy with an average target of A$191 (164% upside). The Melio B2B payments acquisition is approaching breakeven 6-12 months ahead of schedule. The gap between what the business is doing and what the market is pricing has never been wider. This is either the opportunity of the cycle or the market correctly front-running a paradigm shift that hasn't yet appeared in any metric.

WTC.heroDescription: Global Logistics Software • ~17,000 Customers • 174 Countries • e2open Acquired
WTC.heroCompanyDescription: WiseTech Global (ASX: WTC) is the world's leading logistics technology company, operating the CargoWise platform – the dominant operating system for international freight forwarding, customs brokerage, and supply chain management across approximately 17,000 organisations in 174 countries. The August 2025 acquisition of e2open for US$2.4bn transformed WiseTech from a pure-play logistics SaaS company into a broader supply chain platform, with FY26 revenue guidance reaffirmed at US$1.39–1.44bn following 1H26 results that showed US$672M in revenue and US$50M in synergies achieved 18 months ahead of schedule.

skew.rationale: 58% combined weight on upside scenarios (N1 Franchise Intact at 28% + N2 Restructuring Recovery at 30%). Evidence-weighted price A$59 vs market A$47.10. 1H26 results confirmed e2open tracking within guidance and delivered a restructuring catalyst the market was not pricing. Primary downside scenario (covenant breach) materially weakened.

verdict.text: WiseTech is trading at A$39.42, approximately at fair value on a probability-weighted basis given the updated hypothesis distribution – a significant change from the prior assessment of a 23% discount to fair value. The DSV defection to Tango and the ASIC investigation escalation have materially narrowed the prior mispricing by adding new downside risks that the market was arguably correct to price. The CEO's A$1M on-market purchase is the strongest signal that management believes the market is wrong, but one insider's conviction does not resolve the structural questions about CargoWise franchise durability or the legal risk from the ASIC investigation. The July 2026 covenant test remains the most binary near-term catalyst – a clean pass removes the N4 tail risk and likely triggers a re-rating toward A$45–50, while a breach at 3.5x+ would validate the unravelling scenario and push the stock toward A$20–25. At current prices, the risk/reward is approximately balanced, with the CEO purchase and restructuring execution providing the primary upside optionality.

WTC.featuredRationale: 25% gap widened by 1H26 results. Restructuring + synergy beat create credible path to 48-50% margins by FY28. CW organic growth at 9% is the one red flag. August FY26 results are the discriminator.

hypotheses.title: N1: Franchise Intact, Acquisition Works

This scenario requires both the franchise and the acquisition to work simultaneously. The DSV shift to Tango and the ASIC investigation into Richard White are the primary obstacles to this hypothesis gaining weight at current prices.

Existing customer growth fell sharply: US$20.3M vs US$44.7M prior period
Governance unresolved: White's 36% control, ASIC investigation active, no board reconstitution
No active accumulation at current levels despite 50%+ discount to broker targets
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N1 is the only scenario where both the franchise and the acquisition work. It requires the 9% organic growth to be transitional (Value Pack timing) rather than structural. If CW growth reaccelerates to 12%+ in 2H26, N1 regains the weight it lost today. If it stays at 9%, the compounder thesis that justifies the premium multiple in this scenario is damaged.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The 9% organic growth. Every other data point in the 1H26 results was either in line or better than expected. This was worse. The distinction between Value Pack timing disruption and genuine franchise deceleration cannot be resolved before August.

hypotheses.title: N2: Restructuring Recovery
hypotheses.statusText: New Scenario: Upgrade story not yet priced
hypotheses.description: The aggressive cost-out programme (2,000-person restructuring, US$160–240M annualised savings) combines with early synergy delivery to push FY28 EBITDA toward US$800–850M. CargoWise franchise remains broadly intact despite the DSV defection. Margin recovery is the primary value driver, and the CEO's A$1M share purchase supports internal confidence in this path.

Product quality, release cadence, and customer experience are not impaired by 50% headcount reduction
AI tools replace human coding and customer service functions as management claims
July 2026 covenant test passes at ≤3.3× removing tail risk
supporting: US$50M synergy target delivered 18 months ahead of schedule: management track record on cost execution is now established
2,000-person restructuring across product, dev, and customer service: US$160–240M gross annualised savings at US$80–120K blended cost per role
At 60% realisation, US$96–144M incremental EBITDA from FY27: transforms the margin trajectory from mid-40s grind to 48–50% by FY28
Covenant headroom improved by +0.3–0.5×: synergy beat and restructuring savings add meaningful buffer to July test
FY28 EBITDA path US$800–850M: three to four times larger cost-out than guidance implied
contradicting: Cutting 50% of product and development headcount is aggressive: if AI tools are not ready, product quality or release cadence could suffer
FY26 impact guided as neutral: execution costs offset savings in the near term; no visible margin improvement until FY27
Customer service headcount reduction could impair implementation quality for new LGFF customers
Restructuring cost estimates are management-sourced; independent verification will not be available until FY27 results
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N2 did not exist before 25 February 2026. It was created by a single day's disclosure. The Integration Tax scenario (N3) assumed FY28 EBITDA of approximately US$714M at a 44.5% margin. Add US$100–140M of restructuring savings and you reach US$800–850M at 48–50% margins. That is the difference between a stock that goes nowhere for three years and one that re-rates as the margin recovery becomes visible in FY27 reporting.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The AI replacement thesis. Management frames the restructuring as AI-driven transformation. If the AI tools are not ready, the company has eliminated 2,000 people across product development and customer service without a replacement capability. The risk is real and unquantifiable until the next product cycle. No external observer can assess whether WiseTech's internal AI tooling can replace the output of 2,000 engineers and service staff.

hypotheses.title: N3: Integration Tax
hypotheses.statusText: Base Case: Confirmed but exit path created
hypotheses.description: The e2open acquisition creates limited upside while the DSV defection signals potential CargoWise franchise erosion. Margins grind slowly back toward the mid-40s over three years. CargoWise grows at single digits as management attention is divided and competitive pressure from Tango and others intensifies. The stock stagnates as the market refuses to re-rate without clear evidence of franchise re-acceleration.
requires: Restructuring underdelivers: less than 50% of gross savings realised due to execution complexity
CargoWise organic growth remains at 9–11%, below the 14% guided floor
Blended margins recover slowly from 38% toward mid-40s, not the 48–50% in N2
Covenant passes but without excess headroom (3.3–3.5×)
supporting: 1H26 blended EBITDA margin of 38%: e2open dilution confirmed as expected
CW organic growth at 9%: below guidance, consistent with integration distraction
Existing customer growth fell sharply from US$44.7M to US$20.3M: management attention may be splitting
Underlying NPAT flat at US$114.5M (+2%): integration tax at work, earnings going nowhere
contradicting: US$50M synergies achieved 18 months early: management executing faster than base case assumes
Restructuring programme announced: if even partially successful, N3 migrates toward N2
CargoWise gross margin flat at 87%: core franchise economics not impaired by integration
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N3 and N2 start from the same position. The difference is execution on the restructuring programme. N3 assumes 50% or less of gross savings materialise. The FY27 half-year results become the discriminating event: if blended EBITDA margins are tracking above 42%, weight migrates from N3 to N2. Below 40%, N3 solidifies as the dominant scenario.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: N3 is inherently unstable. It describes a transition state, not an equilibrium. Over 12–18 months, the evidence will push weight either toward N2 (restructuring works) or toward a worse version of N3 where integration failure becomes structural. The vulnerability is that N3 resolves itself rather than persists.

hypotheses.title: N4: Reflexive Unravelling
hypotheses.description: The ASIC investigation into Richard White escalates to formal charges, triggering a governance crisis that accelerates institutional exodus. Simultaneously, e2open deteriorates materially, the July 2026 covenant test is breached at 3.5x+ leverage, and lenders demand repayment, forcing an emergency equity raise at a steep discount. The DSV defection signals broader CargoWise customer dissatisfaction, accelerating revenue deterioration.
requires: July 2026 covenant test fails at >3.5× debt-to-earnings
e2open revenue deteriorates beyond the current annualised ~US$550M guidance
Restructuring execution fails completely with no realised savings
Credit markets tighten or lenders refuse waiver
supporting: Pre-results covenant headroom was razor-thin: approximately 0.1×, one bad quarter from breach
US$2.4bn acquisition debt at ~2.9× leverage remains structurally elevated
Statutory NPAT declined 36% to US$68.1M, reflecting US$66M interest + US$38M acquired amortisation
contradicting: US$50M synergies add +0.3× headroom: achieved, not projected
Restructuring savings add +0.1–0.2× in 2H26: partial benefit begins before July test
Combined headroom now +0.3–0.5×: covenant pass at ≤3.3× takes debt crisis off the table
e2open tracking within guidance: annualised ~US$550M, no deterioration signal
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N4 was a 20% probability scenario before these results. It lost 8 percentage points in a single day because the two buffers against covenant breach (synergies achieved, restructuring announced) both materialised simultaneously. If the July test comes in at 3.3× or below, N4 is effectively dead and the market can stop pricing the risk of a capital raise.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: An exogenous shock. N4 now requires something management cannot control: a credit market freeze, a counterparty failure that disrupts refinancing, or e2open revenue collapse. The probability is low precisely because the endogenous risks (execution, synergies) have been addressed. Residual N4 weight reflects tail risk, not central tendency.

identity.overview: WiseTech Global (ASX: WTC) operates CargoWise, the dominant global platform for freight forwarding, customs brokerage, and supply chain management. Founded in 1994 by Richard White, the company serves approximately 17,000 organisations across 174 countries. The August 2025 acquisition of e2open for US$2.4bn transformed WiseTech from a pure-play logistics SaaS company into a broader supply chain platform. 1H26 total revenue of US$672M reflected five months of e2open contribution (US$249.4M) alongside CargoWise revenue of US$372.4M (+12%, 9% organic). Richard White holds 36% of issued capital and returned as Executive Chairman in February 2025 following a governance crisis that saw four board members depart. ASIC investigation into the founder remains unresolved.

GYG.heroCompanyDescription: Guzman y Gomez (ASX: GYG) is an Australian fast-casual restaurant chain specialising in Mexican-inspired food with a clean-ingredient positioning. The company operates 256 restaurants (FY25) across Australia, Singapore, Japan, and the United States, with 108 approved sites in the pipeline. Restaurant-level economics are strong (22% margins, 48-53% franchisee ROI), but comparable sales have decelerated from 15% (FY23) to 4-5% (H1 FY26). US operations are loss-making (A$13.2M EBITDA loss FY25) with the parent entity having written off its entire A$28.2M US investment.

skew.rationale: 72% combined weight on downside scenarios (N2 Decelerating Growth at 46% + N3 US Capital Destruction at 21% + N4 Franchise Stress at 5%). Three consecutive negative filing-day reactions confirm the pattern.

verdict.text: GYG is trading at A$17.53, 29% above the evidence-weighted price of approximately A$13.59. The market assigns 47% probability to the Australian Compounder narrative; the evidence supports 28%. Three consecutive half-year results have produced escalating negative filing-day reactions (−9.2%, −13.9%, −17.6%), and comparable sales have decelerated from 15% (FY23) to 4–5% (H1 FY26). Unit economics remain strong: drive-thru AUV of A$6.9M, restaurant-level margins of 22%, and franchisee ROI of 48–53%. The tension is real: excellent unit economics inside a decelerating growth trajectory. US operations are actively destroying value (A$13.2M EBITDA loss FY25, losses doubling annually), and insider control (44%) prevents course correction. The H1 FY26 results will resolve more uncertainty in a single day than months of analysis can provide. WAIT for results.

verdict.text: At A$50.23, BHP is pricing a copper supercycle that is now consensus rather than contrarian – copper at US$5.71/lb has validated the strategic thesis, but the 26.4% stock rally means the easy re-rating has been completed. The HY2026 results confirmed operational resilience with ~US$27B EBITDA and iron ore costs at US$15-17/t, providing a solid earnings floor. The CEO transition to Brandon Craig (announced 17 March 2026) introduces a new variable: markets will scrutinise whether the incoming CEO maintains Mike Henry's copper-first conviction and M&A discipline. The stock sits near the upper end of its 52-week range (A$34.16–A$59.25), suggesting further upside requires either copper to push materially higher, Jansen to deliver on schedule, or Craig to articulate a compelling strategic vision that extends the re-rating. At current prices, the risk/reward is balanced rather than asymmetrically bullish.

The completed SPP has reduced near-term funding risk, but the pre-revenue status and retail-dominated register continue to suppress institutional price discovery. The risk/reward at current levels appears skewed to the upside: the gold price tailwind is exceptional, the production pathway is now validated, and the stock trades at developer multiples despite early-stage production activity. The key re-rating catalyst is Boorara mining approvals in Q2 2026, which would confirm sustained ore supply and trigger a transition to producer multiples.

verdict.text: FY24 (June year-end) delivered revenue of $10.2B (up 8%), underlying EBITA of $720M (up 12%), and a record backlog of $16.8B. hypotheses.statusText: Priced: Recovery embedded in current P/B

requires: RBA does not reverse course (existing cuts permanent)
Credit losses at or below 1H26 levels (annualised A$19M)
Securitisation market access continues uninterrupted
Organic home loan AUM grows 5–6% annually
ASIC proceedings resolve without material penalty

hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N1 is the only scenario where the franchise is intact and the cycle was the entire problem. The 5–6% organic growth assumption is above FY25's 4% but far below the 25% sector rate. Over three to five years, a lender growing at one-quarter of the sector rate becomes progressively less relevant to brokers, less able to support programmatic securitisation at current scale, and less attractive to the RMBS investor base.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: N1 assumes a franchise earning above cost of equity can sustain that performance while losing market share every period. The risk is that the franchise itself is the problem, and the cycle is merely providing a temporary reprieve. If FY26 organic AUM growth stays below 5% with the sector above 15%, N1 loses its most important requirement.

hypotheses.title: N2: Structurally Narrowing, Macro-Masked
hypotheses.description: 1H26 is the cyclical peak, not the recovery start. Competitive erosion masked by rate-driven NIM expansion. FY26 looks identical to N1 initially (EBIT A$69M); divergence emerges FY27 onward as rate tailwind fades. NIM compresses under competitive pressure; organic AUM growth stagnates while competitors grow at 6x the rate. Three-year path delivers FY28 net income of A$13M and ROE of 3.6%.
requires: Competitive dynamics reassert once rate tailwind stabilises
NIM compresses as rate benefit fades under competitive pressure (161bps → 122bps by FY28)
Impairment normalises above 1H26 levels (A$27M base case)
Management fails to deliver strategy addressing scale disadvantage

Cost-to-income deteriorating: 48.8% → 53.6% vs Liberty at 28%; negative operating leverage (Opex +21% vs NII +7%)

contradicting: APRA DTI cap (Feb 2026) could redirect sufficient volume to offset narrowing

hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: FY26 looks like N1. EBIT of A$69M captures residual rate-cut benefit and SG&A normalisation, producing a result that appears to confirm the cyclical recovery thesis. The divergence from N1 begins in FY27. By FY28, underlying ROE of 8.6% is below cost of equity, the same return environment that the market priced at 0.68–0.77× book for two years. The N2 earnings path is designed to be indistinguishable from N1 at the first observation point and divergent at the second.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The APRA DTI cap. If the volume of redirected borrowers is large enough, even a subscale operator like Resimac could capture sufficient volume to offset the competitive erosion. The DTI cap is a structural demand expansion that N2 acknowledges but underweights. It changes the size of the market rather than the allocation within it.

hypotheses.title: N3: Capital Extraction, Declining Franchise
hypotheses.description: Controlling shareholder's capital allocation is the organising principle. Cumulative dividends exceed cumulative net income by A$60M over three years. Equity eroding from A$365M toward A$290M. Related party lending escalating (A$8M FY24, A$31M FY25, A$25M 1H26). Distributions of A$161M at 4.7x annual earnings concurrent with 4% organic growth suggest extraction over reinvestment. Three-year path sees equity/assets ratio drop to 1.8%.
requires: Controlling shareholder prioritises distributions over reinvestment
Related party transactions continue at current escalating volumes
Management does not materially change capital allocation framework
ASIC proceedings result in material penalty (A$20–30M estimated range)
supporting: A$161M returned to shareholders across FY25–1H26 at 4.7× annual earnings
Related party lending escalated: A$8M (FY24) → A$31M (FY25) → A$25M (1H26) to Saville entities
Saville chairs Audit Committee that oversees the related party transactions he initiates; 3 of 5 directors compromised
No return-on-capital metric (ROE, ROIC, ROA) in STI or LTI compensation despite ROE being disclosed as company metric; AUM growth in both creates dilutive acquisition incentive
contradicting: Saville holds 63.56%: bears full economic consequences; 64 cents per dollar lost falls on his bloc
Corporate debt fully repaid; buyback reducing share count rather than offsetting dilution
Distinction between extraction and rational management of low-growth franchise depends on intent assessment
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: Dividends exceed net income in every year, and this is by design. N3 models the dividend as quasi-fixed, set by the controlling shareholder's extraction preference, with the equity base absorbing the residual. The FY28 one-offs (A$25M ASIC settlement, A$22M full goodwill impairment) are not surprises in this world. They are the crystallisation of risks that the capital allocation framework chose not to provision for.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The alignment argument. Saville holds 63.56% of the equity. Every dollar of lost market capitalisation costs him 64 cents. The strongest counter-argument: the controlling shareholder is not extracting value but rationally managing a company with limited reinvestment opportunities, returning surplus capital that would otherwise earn below cost of equity. The distinction between extraction and rational management of a low-growth franchise is one of intent, and intent cannot be directly observed from financial statements.

hypotheses.title: N4: Strategic Renewal Under New Leadership
hypotheses.description: New CEO's strategy delivers quantified targets with remuneration linkage. Controlling shareholder permits capital retention. Technology and broker investment produce competitive improvement. Three-year path delivers FY28 net income of A$92M and ROE of 19.6%. Requires fundamental shift in capital allocation that contradicts the current shareholder return programme of A$161M at 4.7x earnings.
requires: Controlling shareholder permits capital retention (A$37M+ per annum retained rather than distributed)
Strategy disclosure contains quantified targets (ROE, cost-to-income, organic AUM growth) with remuneration linkage
Organic home loan AUM growth accelerates to 10% (150% acceleration from current 4%)
Technology investment produces measurable competitive improvement (not "agentic AI automation" without benchmarks)
supporting: New CEO appointed April 2025 with mandate for strategic renewal; broad leadership refresh across operations, credit, technology, product, distribution
1H26 underlying ROE of 15.5% demonstrates earnings power when cycle cooperates
APRA DTI cap creates structural opportunity for non-bank lenders to capture redirected flow
contradicting: CEO 10 months into role with no quantified targets disclosed; 72% of forward guidance is directional or vague
Only 1 of ~14 forward statements is testable: Westpac auto portfolio A$12M operating profit (FY26)
A$161M distributed at 4.7× earnings concurrently with strategy development
Controlling shareholder veto: Saville receives 63.56% of every dollar distributed; all operational assumptions contingent on his consent to retain capital
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N4 is the only scenario where gross profit grows materially (A$165M to A$219M, up 33%) and the equity base expands rather than stagnates or erodes. The J-curve is visible: FY27 EBIT dips as the ASIC settlement hits, then FY28 surges to A$130M as strategic investment produces competitive improvement on a larger book.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The controlling shareholder veto. Every other condition is within the CEO's operational scope. Capital allocation is not. The decision to retain A$37M+ per annum requires Saville's affirmative consent, and Saville receives 63.56% of every dollar distributed. If Saville concludes that near-term distributions generate more personal value than uncertain franchise appreciation, the reinvestment pathway closes regardless of the strategy's merits. This is a governance risk that sits upstream of every operational assumption.
